title: Letters to the Editor
summary: The city-owned Port of Anchorage has launched a Web site dedicated specifically to providing information about its $227 million redevelopment project. The new site, at www.portofanchorage.org , includes detailed descriptions of the work to be done, construction timelines, as well as information about contract work available and how to bid for that work. A password-protected part of the site that will enable online program and contract management in real time is expected to be added by the end of the month, said Cheryl Coppe, the port's executive administrator for development. Two guide companies join forces Chugach Powder Guides is merging with Class V Whitewater to form a parent company, Chugach Adventure Guides. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: • Fast Cat Rentals LLC, 336-7063, www.fastcatrentals.com . This company specializes in rentals, and does not offer guided tours. The cost is $150 during the week for a 24-hour rental, or $175 over the weekend. There is a $500 damage deposit required on a major credit card for each machine. Rental comes with a helmet, but you need to bring your own winter gear. If you rent two or more machines, a trailer is included. GIRDWOOD AREA • Glacier City Snowmobile Tours , Girdwood, 783-5566, www.snowtours.net . Offers tours in Turnagain Pass, Placer and Twentymile Valley and Johnson Pass. Options include a scenic beginner tour for $140 and the five-hour "Real Deal" tour that accesses glaciers and icebergs for $175. • Alaska Snow Safaris , Girdwood, 783-7669 or 1-888-414-7669, www.snowmobile-alaska.…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Ron Tomblinson of Anchorage writes: "At the Feb. 7 Guns and Hoses-Alaska Aces hockey game, a 7-year-old boy lost his wallet with $45 in it. Bob and Mark Morning Show disc jockey Bob Lester asked the crowd (that) if anyone found the wallet would they please return it. No one did, so Bob asked the Aces fans to each kick in $1 until the $45 was replaced. The $45 goal was reached in about three minutes, and about the same time the wallet was returned. The money raised was donated to the Diabetes Association. Thank you to Bob and the Aces fans for helping this young man." Contributions support mediator training The students and staff of Chinook Elementary School's Mediator Program thank the businesses and individuals who contributed to their recent peacemaker training. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Ferry and Wrangell High School senior Afton Grossardt were recently named Alaska's top youth volunteers for 2004 by the Prudential Spirit of Community Awards -- a nationwide program that recognizes middle and high school students for outstanding volunteerism. The two Alaskans each earned $1,000 awards, engraved silver medallions and an all-expenses paid trip to Washington, D.C., in May to compete nationally. Created in 1995 by Prudential Financial in partnership with the National Association of Secondary School Principals, the awards program is the largest in the nation that recognizes young volunteers. Since it began, the program has lauded more than 50,000 youths at local, state and national levels. Ferry was nominated by Esther Heck, an English teacher at his school. "I almost did a bac…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: If a community believes lack of access to library records is a problem, it should be handled at the local level, Elton said. But Green said the law, as written now, does not allow local governments to address the issue. It requires public libraries to keep the records confidential. "They will now be allowed to share library information with parents," Green said. "I think that's only fair." "We're not going into something and creating something more restrictive," she said. "We're actually loosening up the law." The change would make public library policy on children's records consistent with what is required in school libraries, Green said. During committee hearings on the bill, an Alaska Library Association representative said the group would like to see the measure amended so that library…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: "It's definitely an enhancement to that area out there because it's bringing in life that wasn't out there before," said Dave Mitchell, a local dive instructor and co-owner of The Scuba Tank. "There's definitely more life. I've been diving here for 16 years and have dived Auke Rec. hundreds of times and there's more life by the Rikki Tikki than I've ever seen out there." Almost immediately after the sinking, large quantities of king crab took up residence in the once derelict boat donated by Trucano Construction, Lachelt said. In his 50 to 70 dives on the Rikki Tikki since October, Lachelt said, he has seen sculpins, decorator crabs, flounders, small halibut, cod, shrimp and needle fish take a liking to the artificial reef. He said he expects drastic changes and much more marine life in th…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Of these 31 occupations only 20 could be found in published U.S. Department of Labor documents for both Alaska and Washington. (Wage rates are not published for a state if there are too few workers in a given occupation.) Not surprisingly, 16 of the 20 occupations for which median hourly wage rates were published for both states showed higher median wage rates paid in Alaska than in Washington. The differentials ranged as high as 26 percent. If Alaska wage rates were not generally higher than elsewhere and if qualified Alaskans were available for hire, rational employers would tend to prefer residents to nonresidents for the simple reason that it is riskier to hire a nonresident than it is to hire a resident. When a company hires a nonresident it incurs all the usual risks of hiring someon…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: "Environmental permitting is a core purpose of government," said Ernesta Ballard, DEC commissioner. "It should be performed on the state level." Last month, on the opening day of the legislative session, Ballard's department gave lawmakers a 73-page report outlining how it would run the program and what it would cost. The state spent $216,000 to prepare the report, according to DEC. The agency would have to hire 14 more staffers and raise an additional $1.7 million to run the $4.8 million program. Some of the money would come from the state treasury. The rest would come from permit fees paid by industry. The Legislature would decide how to split the burden. The response to DEC's plan has been overwhelmingly negative. Groups ranging from the Alaska Oil and Gas Association to the Alaska Mine…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Alaska Digest (Published: February 18, 2004) A black wolf watches passing hikers and dogs on the shore of Mendenhall Lake in Juneau on Tuesday. The young wolf has been hanging around the lake and the Mendenhall Glacier Visitors Center for several months, and officials fear it has become too friendly with humans and dogs. Researchers are not sure why the animal became separated from its pack. Ideally, the wolf will go back to the wild and avoid humans, said Dennis Chester, a wildlife biologist for the Juneau Ranger District of the Forest Service. If it doesn't, biologists will consider whether trapping the animal and relocating it is feasible. "It's kind of like a half-domesticated wolf at this point," Chester said. (Photo by ANDREW KRUEGER / The Associated Press) Click on photo to enlarge …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Also Wednesday, 9-year-old Ryder Doxsee, who survived 17 hours in the cold after the truck his father was driving drove off the ice into the water, was released from the hospital, where he was treated for hypothermia and frostbitten feet. His father, 55-year-old Adrien, died at the lake of exposure. Ryder's mother, Autumn, 42, and two teenage passengers, all of them in a second vehicle that went through the ice, walked to safety. Troopers say the family was ferrying supplies between their Skilak Lake cabin and their Kenai home when the accidents occurred. The two vehicles -- a GMC pickup and a rented U-Haul -- were traveling about an hour and a half apart and went into the lake at different locations in the middle of the night Monday. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: DESCRIPTION: An unincorporated town in the Kenai Peninsula Borough that is the site of several oil refineries. Here North Slope oil is processed into gasoline for Tesoro, Mapco and other gasoline retailers. The Port Nikiski docks service offshore drilling platforms. Timber, commercial and sportfishing, government, retail businesses and tourism-related services also provide employment. More than 55 residents hold commercial fishing permits. There are three schools in the community, attended by about 970 students. One of every 10 residents is Alaska Native. During the April 2000 U.S. census, 1,652 residents were employed, the median household income was $51,176, per capita income was $20,129 and more than 11 percent of residents were living below the poverty level. "We're the only two (incumbents) in the running, and it seems to be addressed at the Assembly," Van Etten said. Van Etten is not worried. He said the group has little credibility. title: State funds reinstated for 8 VPSOs
summary: KODIAK -- State funding for eight village public safety officers has been reinstated, according to Kodiak Rep. Dan Ogg. The funding for 15 VPSOs across Alaska was cut by Gov. Frank Murkowski in June 2003, reducing state expenditures by $1 million. The state spent $5.3 million on the program last year. The cuts in VPSO positions caused outrage in villages, inciting the Kodiak Area Native Association to set up a plan to continue funding five positions on Kodiak Island. The plan, which received funds from the Alaska Native Sobriety and Alcohol Control Program, was only temporary, however. Recent discussions between KANA and the Alaska Department of Public Safety have led to an expanded agreement that will reinstate funding for eight VPSOs. It also will put the Native association in charge of …

summary: More than $4 million would go to pay for increased security on the state's ferries, which have been identified by federal officials as vulnerable to terrorism. The money would go to the state Department of Transportation and to the State Patrol for more troopers, bomb-sniffing dogs and other security measures that have been mandated by the U.S. Coast Guard. "Our ferry system is one of the highest, if not the highest, marine target for a potential marine terrorist attack," said Transportation Chairman Ed Murray, D-Seattle, before House Bill 3205 passed 55-40. title: Alaska Fly Fishers club plans leap into merchandising in big way
summary: Casting about for new fund-raising opportunities, the Alaska Fly Fishers club noted the lingering ripples of the fly-fishing boom, considered the marketability of Alaska and decided there might be a way to bring the two together to produce cold, hard cash. Starting this year, the 500-member club is moving seriously into merchandising. Look for fly boxes, hats, T-shirts, pocket knives and other merchandise adorned with the Alaska Fly Fishers logo coming soon to a fishing shop near you. The Anchorage-based Fly Fishers have dabbled in merchandise for decades. "Fly Patterns of Alaska,'' a book longtime club member Dirk Derksen first pulled together for the organization in the early 1980s, is an Alaska best seller. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Based on their reports, Vonderheide estimated that the object entered Earth's atmosphere somewhere over Montana Creek and moved southwest toward Skwentna before fading from sight. Most meteors are small rocks or grains from space that hurtle into the upper atmosphere 50 to 60 miles above the surface at extraordinary speeds, sometimes more than 100,000 mph. "Globally on any 24-hour night, there's probably several of these (large) fireballs because there's a lot of stuff out there in space," Vonder-heide said. "There's just a lot of rock out there. ... I see two to three of these a year." Thousands of meteors burn up over Earth each day, most above oceans or uninhabited areas or during daylight when they can't be seen, according to a Web site maintained by the American Meteor Society. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: She told troopers that she last spoke to her husband Saturday and that he planned to drive to Mile 16 Kougarok Road, off-load his black Polaris 500 and go to his cabin at Mile 39. Troopers tried to reach the cabin late Sunday but recent snowfall and poor trail conditions prevented travel. Monday morning, troopers organized a ground search and launched two state aircraft. Nome residents and fire department volunteers joined the effort too. Searchers found West about 3 p.m., several hundred yards off Kougarok Road near Mile 34, partially submerged in the shallow creek. Troopers determined he never made it to his cabin. Troopers spokesman Greg Wilkinson said this is the eighth snowmachine-related fatality of the 2003-2004 winter season. summary: The heavy-duty truck, used to haul ore from the mine's open pit to the stockpile area, rolled three times, she said. An investigation is under way, and representatives of Caterpillar Inc., the manufacturer, are at the mine examining the truck, he said. Red Dog officials reported the accident shortly after it happened, said Doug Bowen, Anchorage-based supervisor for the federal Mine Safety and Health Administration, which oversees open-pit mines. They have 10 days from the time of the accident to file a more detailed report, he said. Red Dog is the world's biggest producer of zinc and lead. Podvin, 40, died a year after publicly announcing his sexual abuse by a Catholic priest in a television interview. title: Governor's office behind flip-flop on risky logging road
summary: Alaska environmental regulators lifted their staunch opposition to a proposed logging road in Southeast -- one that could contaminate public drinking water -- after the governor's office encouraged officials to rethink their position. The timber-access road would cut across two public drinking water sources near Saltery Cove, on the east side of Prince of Wales Island, near Ketchikan. The Forest Service proposed the road as part of a federal timber sale, called Cholmondeley. About 10 million board feet of spruce, hemlock and cedar would be cut from the Saltery Cove watershed within the Tongass National Forest. Cholmondeley is the first large-scale timber sale in the Tongass in two years. After much planning and controversy over how the logging would affect wildlife and tourist businesses, …
